selection.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.

I'm out here with my tools, fixing circuits, keeping the lights on in Milan. But I've seen enough of the fuss about this fake stuff, this nonsense they put online, to understand the worry. It's like trying to tell the difference between a proper wire connection and one that's been spliced together poorly—you need to look closely at the work. For me, the best way to deal with this flood of untruths is to keep my distance, which is why I don't use it much. But if one *must* engage, the principle is simple: skepticism, like checking a multimeter reading twice. Don't just take what pops up on the screen as solid fact. If something sounds too dramatic, too perfect, or just plain strange, you treat it like a faulty connection; you don't trust it immediately. You have to ask, who is saying this? What is their motive? Are they trying to sell me something, or are they just trying to make a ruckus? My experience with things that are not right, whether it's a bad splice or a dishonest word, tells me that the source is everything. You need a solid foundation, a reliable source, before you build your belief on top of it. It reminds me a bit of how I approach my cooking; you can't just throw spices in without knowing what they are supposed to do. You need to know the recipe, the ingredients, the truth of it all. Because when you are feeling low, and the world outside feels messy, these lies can just pile on and make everything darker, like a power surge you can't trace. response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.
response_parsed.opinion: It sounds like you are asking about this 'social media' business, and honestly, I don't spend my time on those things much. I'm out here with my tools, fixing circuits, keeping the lights on in Milan. But I've seen enough of the fuss about this fake stuff, this nonsense they put online, to understand the worry. It's like trying to tell the difference between a proper wire connection and one that's been spliced together poorly—you need to look closely at the work. For me, the best way to deal with this flood of untruths is to keep my distance, which is why I don't use it much. But if one *must* engage, the principle is simple: skepticism, like checking a multimeter reading twice. Don't just take what pops up on the screen as solid fact. If something sounds too dramatic, too perfect, or just plain strange, you treat it like a faulty connection; you don't trust it immediately. You have to ask, who is saying this? What is their motive? Are they trying to sell me something, or are they just trying to make a ruckus? My experience with things that are not right, whether it's a bad splice or a dishonest word, tells me that the source is everything. You need a solid foundation, a reliable source, before you build your belief on top of it. It reminds me a bit of how I approach my cooking; you can't just throw spices in without knowing what they are supposed to do. You need to know the recipe, the ingredients, the truth of it all. Because when you are feeling low, and the world outside feels messy, these lies can just pile on and make everything darker, like a power surge you can't trace. So, the management isn't about fighting every single lie; it's about building up your own internal circuit board of trust, and keeping the unreliable signals out.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: The persona is an older, depressed, low-extraversion man with primary education who is not on social media. His traits suggest a cautious, skeptical, and perhaps slightly overwhelmed worldview. The topic is managing fake content. The response must reflect his practical, hands-on mindset (electrician/cooking analogies) while acknowledging his psychological state (depressed/high neuroticism). Since he doesn't use social media, the advice must be philosophical and cautionary rather than tactical. The tone should be world-weary, pragmatic, and slightly resigned.
